caveats: a: Broad machinery occupation proxy.
a: OEM sophistication and plant automation vary widely.
rho: No observed code-specific implementation rate.
rho: Safety-critical outputs need qualified review.
e: Injected firm count is margin-bridged and estimated.
e: Industry classification does not establish recurring outsourced economics.
s5: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
s5: No public code-specific transfer denominator.
q: No public contract evidence measures pass-through.
q: Aftermarket parts and proprietary attachments likely retain more than build-to-print components.
d5: Farm income is a demand driver, not equipment quantity.
d5: Large-crop, livestock, specialty-crop, and turf equipment cycles differ.
o: Operator share can migrate to large OEMs or foreign suppliers.
o: This is distinct from labor automation within the manufacturer.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B equipment and parts sales through dealers, OEM programs, direct farm accounts, and replacement cycles, with engineering, configuration, warranty, parts, and technical support.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive production, dealerships without manufacturing, repair-only businesses, financing vehicles, shell entities, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers of farm, agricultural, turf, and grounds-care machinery, implements, attachments, replacement assemblies, and repeat engineered equipment sold to external dealers, farms, landscapers, or OEMs.

a.bridge: BLS occupation material covers machinery manufacturing broadly rather than six-digit 333111. It identifies assemblers and fabricators, welders, and related physical production work. I map engineering-document search, options configuration, quoting, CAD assistance, work instructions, demand and parts forecasting, scheduling, procurement, warranty triage, and dealer support as exposed; welding, machining, assembly, painting, inspection, testing, maintenance, and material handling are less substitutable. This may overstate exposure in small production-heavy implement plants.
caveats: Broad machinery occupation proxy.
OEM sophistication and plant automation vary widely.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted occupation and task inventories from 12-15 LMM farm-equipment makers, including current CAD/CAM, ERP, dealer, and service systems, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can improve complex information flows around configurable products, but most manufacturing tasks remain physical and equipment-bound.

d5.bridge: USDA forecasts real 2026 net farm income down 2.6% from 2025, while Federal Reserve data directly track 333111 production but do not supply a five-year forecast. I treat current farm economics as a weak cyclical starting point and allow replacement demand and a recovery toward normal equipment purchasing over five years. This can overstate recovery if commodity margins remain compressed or understate it if the machinery cycle turns sharply.
caveats: Farm income is a demand driver, not equipment quantity.
Large-crop, livestock, specialty-crop, and turf equipment cycles differ.
d5.change_evidence: USDA farm-income scenarios, Association of Equipment Manufacturers unit sales, dealer inventories, equipment age, and five-year OEM order forecasts split by product class would materially revise the range.
d5.rationale: Farm equipment demand is cyclical and currently pressured by farm profitability, but replacement needs, labor scarcity, precision features, and normal capital cycles support a five-year recovery case.

caveats: Injected firm count is margin-bridged and estimated.
Industry classification does not establish recurring outsourced economics.
e.change_evidence: Classify the injected 233 candidates by ownership, actual manufacturing, repeat revenue, aftermarket mix, EBITDA, dealer reach, and customer concentration using business databases and interviews.
e.rationale: Many independent LMM manufacturers sell repeat products, parts, and programs externally, but captive OEM plants, low-repeat custom fabricators, dealership-heavy firms, extreme customer concentration, weak aftermarket economics, and brands dependent on a single product cycle reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333111 ($4,995,765,919) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333111 ($42,969,454,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $634,235 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333111); class <5: 305 firms, avg revenue/firm $649,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $127,378 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 199 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,710,668 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $335,633 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 165 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,890,018 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $763,222 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 233 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,356,639 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,620,573 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 85 firms, avg revenue/firm $65,210,024 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,794,207 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $895,519,351 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $175,700,897 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 233

caveats: Operator share can migrate to large OEMs or foreign suppliers.
This is distinct from labor automation within the manufacturer.
o.change_evidence: Dealer and farm surveys on imports, direct sourcing, on-farm fabrication, autonomous equipment adoption, and OEM vertical integration would move the estimate.
o.rationale: Retained demand requires an accountable manufacturer to engineer, fabricate, assemble, test, warrant, and support physical machines and parts. Software and autonomy change product features and service channels but do not eliminate machinery manufacture; imports and customer self-fabrication can shift supplier share.

caveats: No public contract evidence measures pass-through.
Aftermarket parts and proprietary attachments likely retain more than build-to-print components.
q.change_evidence: Dealer agreements, OEM contracts, discount histories, warranty economics, price-cost bridges, competitive win/loss data, and customer interviews would directly test retention.
q.rationale: Product differentiation, dealer relationships, installed base, parts compatibility, and fixed-price equipment allow meaningful retention. Dealer discounts, competitive bids, OEM cost-downs, farmer price sensitivity, and cyclical discounting pass some savings through.

caveats: No observed code-specific implementation rate.
Safety-critical outputs need qualified review.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots across dealer support, quote-to-order, engineering change, warranty triage, parts forecasting, and production planning at representative firms would move the range.
rho.rationale: Quoting, configuration, documentation, parts identification, planning, warranty support, and engineering assistance can be deployed within five years. Safety validation, seasonal peaks, fragmented dealer data, legacy systems, physical prototypes, and product-liability review constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age chartbooks cover small businesses generally rather than farm-machinery control transfers. I use owner age as succession pressure and discount for internal succession, closures, minority investments, OEM-owned firms, and non-transferable founder or dealer relationships. Strategic consolidation may raise realized transfers, while the agricultural cycle can delay them.
caveats: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
No public code-specific transfer denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-based PitchBook/CapIQ/Grata review of 333111 ownership changes and closures since 2018 plus agricultural-equipment M&A adviser interviews would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: A sizeable LMM population, succession, and strategic interest support deals, but cyclic earnings, product liability, dealer dependence, and family ownership reduce five-year qualifying transfers.

principal_driver: A large installed base and repeat dealer, parts, engineering, and support workflows create durable relationships and automatable knowledge work.
principal_weakness: Cyclical capital spending and production-heavy operations constrain realized labor savings and demand visibility.
